items.q: Is diversification the safest growth strategy because it spreads risk?
items.correct: No - in Ansoff's matrix diversification is the riskiest option: the business faces an unfamiliar product AND an unfamiliar market at the same time.
items.misconception: Yes - spreading into new areas always reduces overall risk.
items.random_wrong: Yes - diversification means selling more of what you already make.
items.why: Portfolio logic (don't put eggs in one basket) collides with Ansoff's logic. For a single strategic move, double unfamiliarity means double the ways to fail.

items.q: Does a differentiation strategy mean charging lower prices than rivals?
items.correct: No - differentiation competes by being distinctive in ways customers value, which often supports a PREMIUM price; competing on price is the low-cost strategy.
items.misconception: Yes - being different means being cheaper.
items.random_wrong: Yes - differentiation refers to paying staff different wages.
items.why: Students collapse all competition into price. The two generic routes are opposites: win by spending less than rivals, or win by being worth more to customers.

items.q: Are market development and new product development the same thing?
items.correct: No - market development takes EXISTING products to new markets; new product development creates NEW products for existing markets.
items.misconception: Yes - both just mean growing the business, so they are interchangeable.
items.random_wrong: Market development means inventing new products.
items.why: The similar names invite swapping. Fix each to its axis of Ansoff's matrix: which element changes - the market or the product?

items.q: In Ansoff's matrix, selling existing products in new markets is called:
items.why: Correct answer: Market development. “Market penetration” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Increasing market share of existing products in existing markets is:
items.why: It's Market penetration. “Diversification” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A business competing by offering something distinctive that customers value, rather than the lowest price, is pursuing:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A differentiation strategy. “A low-cost strategy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many growth strategies are shown in Ansoff's matrix?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 strategies. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
